g.data-narration: Welcome to Operator Precedence in Java - the essential rules that determine how expressions are evaluated.
g.text: university - BCS306A: Object-Oriented Programming with Java

g.data-narration: Operator precedence defines the hierarchy determining which operator gets evaluated first when multiple operators appear in a single expression.
g.text: A set of rules that determines the order in which operators are evaluated
when multiple operators are present in an expression.

g.data-narration: Without precedence rules, expressions like 2 + 3 * 4 would be ambiguous. Java defines 15 precedence levels to resolve such ambiguity.
g.text: Java has 15 levels of precedence. Parentheses are highest (level 15),
assignment is lowest (level 2). This ensures every expression has a

g.data-narration: Here's the complete precedence hierarchy in Java from highest to lowest. Memorize this order for exam success.
Postfix operators expr++ and expr-- have the highest precedence among operators. Unary operators like ++expr and --expr come next.
Multiplicative operators (* / %) are evaluated before additive operators (+ -). This is standard math rules.
Shift operators (<< >> >>>) come next, followed by relational operators (< > <= >= instanceof).
Equality operators (== !=) come after relational. Then bitwise AND, XOR, and OR in that order.
Logical AND (&&) has higher precedence than logical OR (||). Remember: NOT > AND > OR!
g.text: true || false && false → true || false = true

g.data-narration: The ternary operator (? :) has second-lowest precedence. Assignment operators have the lowest precedence in Java.
When operators have the same precedence, associativity determines evaluation order. Most are left-to-right, except unary, ternary, and assignment.
Parentheses always override default precedence. Always use parentheses in critical expressions for clarity and correctness.
g.text: Parentheses () have the HIGHEST precedence - they force evaluation order.
💡 Exam tip: When in doubt, use parentheses! It makes code readable.

g.data-narration: Let's evaluate 10 + 5 * 2. Multiplication has higher precedence, so 5*2=10 is calculated first, then 10+10=20.
For complex expressions like a + b * c - a / b % c, evaluate multiplicative operators left-to-right first.
For logical expressions, evaluate relational operators first, then AND (&&), then OR (||).
Pre-increment (++a) increments first then uses value. Post-increment (a++) uses value then increments.
Remember these exam tips: Multiplicative before Additive, NOT before AND before OR, use parentheses, and watch for integer division!
